food_name: Braised Beef Offal with Banh Mi
aliases: Braised Beef Offal with Banh Mi

english: Braised Beef Offal with Banh Mi

food_subgroup: Braised meat and offal dishes with bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vietnamese braised beef offal with banh mi; mapped to closest beef offal food product class. The dish includes braised tripe, intestines, and lungs in coconut milk broth served with a Vietnamese baguette.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef offal, cooked) used as closest defensible anchor. The full dish includes coconut milk broth and banh mi baguette which are not captured in this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Vietnamese street food dish.

serving.description: 1 bowl of braised offal with 1 baguette
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of slow-braised beef offal in coconut milk broth accompanied by one full Vietnamese baguette for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate from beef tripe cooked (FDC ID 172544), beef intestine cooked (FDC ID 169453), coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and French baguette (FDC ID 174924)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish Phá Lấu Bò Bánh Mì. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries: beef tripe cooked (FDC ID 172544), beef intestine cooked (FDC ID 169453), coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and French baguette (FDC ID 174924), proportioned to reflect the dish composition (approximately 55% offal mix, 20% coconut milk broth, 25% baguette by edible weight). Input record values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and found broadly consistent; the USDA FoodData Central composite is used as the sole primary evidence basis. FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted as secondary regional reference only.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Phá Lấu Bò Bánh Mì. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard threshold of GI ≤ 55 = low) using an analogous-food composite approach: white baguette/French bread GI ≈ 73 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson 2021), weighted against the high-protein, high-fat offal and coconut milk components which substantially blunt glycaemic response. The mixed-meal GI is estimated at approximately 55 based on the macronutrient dampening effect of 8.2 g protein and 8.2 g fat per 100 g. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (45.1 g from 550 g × 8.2 g/100 g) / 100 = 24.8, rounded to 24.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg per 100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1,799 mg sodium, approaching the daily limit of 2,000 mg recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced fish sauce in preparation, as fish sauce is the primary sodium contributor; cumulative daily sodium from other meals must also be accounted for.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The Vietnamese baguette component contributes refined carbohydrate with a high intrinsic GI (~73), though the overall mixed-meal GI is moderated to approximately 55 by the protein (8.2 g/100 g) and fat (8.2 g/100 g) from the offal and coconut milk; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 45 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the baguette portion to half or substitute with a lower-GI bread, monitor post-meal blood glucose, and account for the approximately 45 g carbohydrate load of a full serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 54.6 mg/100 g (approximately 300 mg per full 550 g serving, at the upper daily limit), saturated fat at 4.6 g/100 g from coconut milk, and high sodium (327 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced coconut milk and fish sauce, keep the portion to 200–250 g, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and cholesterol load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef offal (especially intestines) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; however, the dish also contains evidence-based anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, star anise, lemongrass, garlic).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit portion size to reduce arachidonic acid intake, request omission of chilli if solanine-sensitive, and consume this dish infrequently given the cumulative saturated fat from coconut milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phá Lấu Bò Bánh Mì (Braised Beef Offal with Banh Mi) is a classic Vietnamese street food originating in southern Vietnam and widely popular in Ho Chi Minh City and among Vietnamese communities in Singapore. The dish features a slow-braised mixture of beef offal — typically tripe, intestines, and lungs — simmered for several hours in a richly spiced coconut milk broth seasoned with fish sauce, lemongrass, star anise, cinnamon, garlic, and chilli. The long braising process renders the offal tender and deeply flavoured, with a silky, aromatic sauce that carries the warmth of the spices and the creaminess of the coconut milk. The dish is served with a crusty Vietnamese baguette (bánh mì), which is used to scoop and dip into the broth. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 136 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 8.2 g fat (4.6 g saturated from coconut milk), and 8.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notable at 327 mg/100 g, primarily from fish sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is elevated at approximately 55 mg/100 g due to the offal content. The mixed-meal glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content despite the high-GI baguette component. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (wheat baguette) or fish (fish sauce). It is culturally significant as a street food snack or light meal and is commonly consumed in the morning or as an afternoon snack in Vietnamese food culture.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete dish Phá Lấu Bò Bánh Mì. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central composite of beef tripe, beef intestine, coconut milk, and French baguette, cross-checked against the input GrabFood ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The composite approach introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite: beef tripe cooked (FDC ID 172544), beef intestine cooked (FDC ID 169453), coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), French baguette (FDC ID 174924), weighted approximately 55% offal mix, 20% coconut milk broth, 25% baguette by edible weight. Input GrabFood values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were broadly consistent and used as a cross-check only; USDA FoodData Central composite is the sole primary evidence basis. FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted as secondary regional reference only and not used as primary evidence. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture values for this composite mixed dish (braised beef offal in coconut milk broth with baguette) are not reliably derivable at the required precision from available approved sources; USDA FoodData Central (individual component entries) and FAO/INFOODS SEA table were consulted but do not provide a directly applicable composite entry for this preparation. 3. GI estimated at 55 (low, GI ≤ 55 threshold) using analogous composite method; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-17. gi_category set to 'low' consistent with GI = 55 per standard classification (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70). 4. Halal status marked false as intestines and lungs require specific halal slaughter certification not confirmed for this dish as sold on GrabFood. 5. food_subgroup corrected from 'Noodle dishes' (input error — dish contains no noodles) to 'Braised meat and offal dishes with bread'. 6. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Vietnam as the dish originates in Vietnam, though it is widely sold in Singapore.
